posts.name: Ch@rlie F|_|
posts.cooked:
What causes the crushed kid vitamin nose in wine?

Seem to get it here and there on burgundy, loire, jura reds.

posts.name: Brent Clayton
posts.cooked: Your palate? You’re going there because that smell conjures up a sensory memory. When I think crushed vitamins I think citric acid/chalk/fruit tones, things you would probably expect to find in some wines.
Not sure. I only get notes of gummy vitamins, so nothing crushed.

posts.name: Mark Ye
posts.cooked:
I get the vitamin and medicinal note on nose

Crushed vitamin is more palate where u get the vitamin/medicinal note along with little hits of sour medicine taste. so it’s like the vitamin pill got crushed into more powder form.

posts.name: Anton Dotson
posts.cooked: I think ‘vitamin’ falls under the banner of sous bois.

posts.name: Doug Schulman
posts.cooked: Crushed adult vitamins or children’s chewables? Barbera and Dolcetto usually have something that reminds me of the Flinstones children’s chewables I took when I was a kid. I have no idea why, or if anyone else smells it.

I know exactly what you are talking about and have wondered the same thing. To me it is a defect, like band-aid aroma from Brett. Someone is gonna have to geek out here and figure out exactly which part or parts of a multivitamin has that aroma.

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
I also tend to get ‘crushed children’s vitamins’ often when trying Italian reds for some reason - have no idea why and I would not consider this a ‘fault’ in any way - though let me go back to my textbooks and see if I can come up with anything.

It’d be interesting to see if this is consistent with a certain region’s wines and then speak to an ‘expert’ in that region to find out if it is ‘common’ for those in the region to pick it out. My guess - unless they are around these types of vitamins now or they were as kids, no.

Cheers

posts.name: Chris V a n d e r m a r e l
posts.cooked:
I do get these types of notes. I’ve even seen some professionals tasting notes refer to ‘crushed tablet’ which I think is similar

I smell/taste something that reminds me of this

I definitely don’t consider it a fault, but I don’t usually like it when I encounter it. To me it feels like a synthetic version of ‘mineral’.
posts.display_username: Chris V a n d e r m a r e l

posts.name: Dave Nerland
posts.cooked: Eating too much caviar causes the crush kid vitamin smell you are getting.

posts.name: Bret Rooks
posts.cooked: When I think of “crushed multi-vitamins,” the first association that comes to mind for me is B-complex vitamins, which (from a quick web search) seem to get their distinctive smell from B1/thiamine, a sulfur-based compound. So perhaps there’s a connection to thiols or something like that? Hopefully, someone who understands chemistry far better than I do can weigh in.

posts.name: Jason L e f l e r
posts.cooked: i have found it often in carignan and zinfandel. sometimes i wonder if it’s the consequence of acid additions but i’m not sure.
posts.display_username: Jason L e f l e r

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
When I think of “crushed multi-vitamins,” the first association that comes to mind for me is B-complex vitamins, which (from a quick web search) seem to get their distinctive smell from B1/thiamine, a sulfur-based compound. So perhaps there’s a connection to thiols or something like that? Hopefully, someone who understands chemistry far better than I do can weigh in.

Interesting - thiamine is a pretty important amino acid used during fermentation. It’s also been shown that thiamine is a ‘food source’ preferred by brett, so left over thiamine after primary fermentation may not be a good thing. I am NOT saying that the vitamin smell is brett-based - I do not believe that is the case. And could it be a thiol thing? Perhaps - many sulfur related compounds tend to ‘blow off’ with O2 but this smell does not to me.

Cheers

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
I have honestly no idea what you’re talking about.

I’ve crushed lots of pills and tablets in my life and can’t recall a single one of them having a distinctive smell, let alone one I could pick up in a wine. Must be an American thing, this.

posts.name: Tom DeBiase
posts.cooked:
What causes the crushed kid vitamin nose in wine?

Seem to get it here and there on burgundy, loire, jura reds.

I don’t get any fruity type flavors from crushed vitamins but do get distinct aged beef notes which are nice when you are going to grill steak but not experiencing it in other food or beverage products. Comes from the b vitamin complex (and thioesters).I assume this is not what you are smelling-tasting.

Tom

posts.name: Jerry Murray
posts.cooked: I attribute this to red wines aged on lees for an extended period of time (too long). It is sometimes interpreted as marmite or vegitmite, again yeast in origin.

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
I attribute this to red wines aged on lees for an extended period of time (too long). It is sometimes interpreted as marmite or vegitmite, again yeast in origin.

Jerry,

Thanks for your insights. And it’s an interesting observation - I keep my wines on fine lees for extended periods of times and I do not get the same characteristics on these.

Cheers

posts.name: Al Osterheld
posts.cooked:
I can happily report I have no idea how crushed Flintstone vitamins smell.

-Al

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
What causes the crushed kid vitamin nose in wine?

Seem to get it here and there on burgundy, loire, jura reds.

Reduction.

You’ll get the same aroma in the fermenting musts when yeasts are nutrient and/or heat stressed.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
Reduction.

You’ll get the same aroma in the fermenting musts when yeasts are nutrient and/or heat stressed.

At least now I know where the note comes from, I just don’t know which one it is. Well, in all likelihood I know it and have noticed it a gazillion times, but since I don’t have any exposure to these kid vitamins, I just can’t relate to the descriptor.

A propos, I’ve been wondering for the longest time this one very Campari-like note in some wines. It’s a fragrant, zesty, citrus-like aroma (I associate it with chinotto, which is the citrus fruit responsible of a good deal of Campari’s heady aroma) I normally pick up in lighter natural red wines from Loire, Jura, Burgundy, Germany and northern Italy. I’ve deduced it must be something that comes with brett or a combination of brett and VA, but I’ve yet to fully understand what this note actually is (on a chemical level).

Is anybody here familiar with that particular note I’m talking about? And this aroma is something I find exclusively in low/no-SO2 wines, so if you are not a natural wine drinker, most likely this is not something you have come across. I want to stress that even though it’s a natural wine aroma, it’s definitely not a fault or flaw, because I find it quite attractive and many times I’ve picked up in wines that show no obvious faults or flaws, not even any other bretty notes - yet when tasting blind, I’ve easily picked up the wine as a bretty naturalista, even when everybody else is commenting on how the wine must be a lovely high-quality Burgundy red (turning out to be something like a naturalist Jura Pinot or Loire Gamay, but lovely all the same).

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
What causes the crushed kid vitamin nose in wine?

Seem to get it here and there on burgundy, loire, jura reds.

Reduction.

You’ll get the same aroma in the fermenting musts when yeasts are nutrient and/or heat stressed.

Not sure it’s that simple - and it does not blow off when I notice it . . .

Cheers.

posts.cooked:

I think just crush a multivitamin or B-vitamin, as others have said, and you’ll understand the smell.
